title: USS Gerald R. Ford departs Middle East after record 10-month deployment
summary: The USS Gerald R. Ford aircraft carrier departed the Middle East on May 2, leaving two carriers (USS Abraham Lincoln and USS George H.W. Bush) maintaining operations in the region including the Iran naval blockade.
contents:
## USS Gerald R. Ford Departs Middle East

### Departure Confirmed
The USS Gerald R. Ford (CVN-78), the US Navy's newest and most advanced aircraft carrier, departed the Middle East on May 2, 2026, ending a record-breaking deployment that exceeded 10 months and 296 days — the longest US carrier deployment since the Vietnam War.

### Deployment History
The Ford departed Naval Station Norfolk on June 24, 2025, initially for operations in Europe. The carrier subsequently operated above the Arctic Circle, in the Caribbean during operations against Venezuela, and in the Middle East supporting Operation Epic Fury against Iran. A fire broke out in the ship's main laundry spaces on March 12, injuring two sailors.

### Remaining Naval Presence
Two aircraft carriers continue operating in the Middle East: the USS Abraham Lincoln (CVN-72) and USS George H.W. Bush (CVN-77). Approximately 20 US Navy vessels remain deployed in the region, maintaining the naval blockade of Iranian ports that began April 13.

### Strategic Context
Defense analyst Mark Cancian of the Center for Strategic and International Studies noted that US military capability "has not changed" despite the Ford's departure. The carrier's crew and ship required rotation after the extended deployment. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth confirmed a second carrier would join the blockade enforcement, with 48 commercial ships redirected so far.
